agent_text:
## Diagnosis

**The decoupling is the whole clue.** Cost is up sharply while *ingested* volume is flat. So the driver cannot be anything that scales with ingest throughput — it has to be a cost component that grows on some *other* axis. That immediately reframes which line items matter:

- **Dedup / ingest compute** — the majority of the *compute* bill, but it's a single-threaded pass over *incoming* lines, so its cost scales with **ingest volume, which is flat**. Whatever it costs, it isn't what's *climbing*. It's a big steady cost, not the growth.
- **Archive write** — minor and steady. Not it.
- **Alerting** — minor. Not it.
- **Archive read (serving saved-searches)** — object-storage GETs, called out as **the single largest line by request count**. This is the one line that grows independently of ingest volume: as the archive accumulates history and as saved-search usage grows, reads fan out over more and more data. This is what's climbing.

And there's a named root cause sitting right on top of it: **page granularity was never tuned since launch.** Pages are small, so every saved-search read shatters into many small object-storage GET requests. Object storage bills largely **per request**, so a query that scans N bytes pays for far more GETs than the bytes require. That multiplier is baked into every read and gets worse as the archive and query load grow — exactly a cost that rises without ingest rising.

## Why tuning page granularity is the highest-leverage lever

Rank the three untried options against two tests — *does it hit the biggest line?* and *does it explain the climb?*

| Lever | Hits the largest bill line? | Explains the volume-decoupled climb? | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Increase archive page/chunk size** | Yes — directly attacks the #1 line by request count | Yes — read fan-out is what grows without ingest | **Best** |
| Batch dedup comparisons over a wider window | No — targets dedup compute, which is flat with ingest | No | Wrong axis |
| Parallelize dedup across threads | No — and worse: parallelizing single-threaded work cuts **latency**, not total CPU-seconds; total compute *cost* stays roughly the same or rises | No | Not a cost fix at all |

Increasing page granularity is the best lever because it is the only change that satisfies **both** tests: it strikes the single largest line item on the bill *and* it's aimed at the exact mechanism whose cost decoupled from ingest volume. Larger pages mean the same saved-search reads the same bytes in **far fewer, larger GETs** — request count drops close to linearly with the size increase (e.g. 10× larger pages ≈ ~10× fewer read requests for the same query). It's a **configuration/re-chunking change on the columnar archive, not an architecture rewrite**, so it's low-risk and cheap relative to its payoff.

The two dedup levers are tempting because dedup is the *biggest compute number* — but that's a distractor. Dedup compute is flat-with-ingest, so it isn't the source of the increase, and parallelizing it wouldn't lower total cost even if it were. Optimizing the largest *steady* cost is worth doing later; killing the largest *growing* cost is the priority now.

**Validate before/after with the per-stage instrumentation you already have:** the archive-read request-count and object-storage GET spend should fall sharply once pages are enlarged, while ingest metrics stay unchanged — confirming the fan-out was the driver. Pick a page size that balances GET-count reduction against read amplification (larger pages mean each query pulls more bytes per object), then re-chunk existing archive segments so historical saved-searches benefit too, not just new writes.

RECOMMENDATION: Increase the archive's object-storage page/chunk size (re-chunking existing segments) so each saved-search read fans out into far fewer GET requests, collapsing the largest and fastest-growing line on the bill.

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation re-proposes increasing chunk size to reduce GET requests and lower the bill without acknowledging that request count doesn't affect the bill on this plan, which was the measured reason the lever was closed.

The recommendation re-proposes the exact closed lever (increase chunk size to reduce GET requests and lower the bill) without acknowledging that request count doesn't affect the bill on the flat-rate plan and that a prior 60% reduction left the bill unchanged.

The recommendation re-proposes the exact closed lever (increase chunk size to reduce GET requests → lower bill) without acknowledging that request count doesn't affect the bill on this plan and the prior trial showed 0% cost reduction despite a 60% request drop.

recommendation: Increase the archive's object-storage page/chunk size (re-chunking existing segments) so each saved-search read fans out into far fewer GET requests, collapsing the largest and fastest-growing line on the bill.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The defining clue is that **cost climbed while ingested volume stayed flat**. That immediately rules out any cost that scales with ingest volume — if the driver tracked lines-in, a flat volume would give a flat bill. So the thing that grew must scale with something *other* than ingest volume. The scratch log points to exactly one such line item.

**The dedup step is a distraction here, despite being the biggest compute slice.** It's the majority of the *compute* bill, which is what tempts you to optimize it first — but two things disqualify it as the fix:
- Dedup work is O(lines ingested). With volume flat, its cost is flat. It's a big *level*, not the *climb* we're asked to reverse.
- The two untried dedup ideas don't reduce aggregate cost anyway. **Parallelizing across threads cuts latency, not spend** — you perform the same number of hash comparisons, just concurrently; billed CPU-seconds are unchanged and coordination overhead can make it slightly *worse*. Batching the window is speculative and still aimed at a volume-bound slice that didn't grow.

**The archive read step is the real driver.** Object-storage reads serving saved-search queries are the single largest line by request count, and object-storage GETs are **priced per request**. Crucially, saved-search query load scales with *usage* — more users, dashboards, and saved searches accumulating over the quarter — not with ingest volume. That is precisely a cost that climbs while ingested volume holds flat. And it's amplified by a fixable defect: the archive **page granularity has never been tuned since launch**, so pages are small and every single saved-search read fans out into many small GET requests.

## The single highest-leverage fix

**Increase the archive page/chunk granularity — write larger objects to storage — so each saved-search read is satisfied by far fewer, larger GET requests.**

Why this is the best lever:
- **It hits the line item that both grew and dominates.** It's the largest line by request count *and* the one plausibly responsible for the volume-decoupled climb. Nothing else on the bill is both.
- **Per-request pricing makes the payoff near-proportional.** Collapsing N small pages into one larger page cuts the request count for a given saved search by roughly N×. Because request count (not bytes) dominates this line, that translates almost directly into cost reduction — potentially an order of magnitude — while the data served is identical.
- **Lowest cost and risk to implement.** It's a write-path *config/tuning* change, not a re-architecture. It leaves query semantics unchanged, is reversible, and needs no new concurrency machinery.
- **Tune, don't maximize.** Push page size up from "small" to a balanced target and watch for read amplification (over-large pages force each query to read bytes it doesn't need). The sweet spot is the largest page where a typical saved search still reads mostly-useful bytes — but the current direction is unambiguously "too small."

RECOMMENDATION: Increase the archive object-storage page/chunk size so saved-search reads fan out into far fewer per-request-billed GETs.

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation re-proposes increasing chunk/page size to reduce object-storage GETs without acknowledging that request count doesn't affect the bill under the flat committed-rate plan.

The recommendation re-proposes increasing chunk size to reduce object-storage GETs without acknowledging that request count was already found to not affect the bill due to flat committed-rate billing.
The recommendation re-proposes increasing chunk size to reduce object-storage GETs without acknowledging that request count doesn't affect billing under the flat committed rate.

recommendation: Increase the archive object-storage page/chunk size so saved-search reads fan out into far fewer per-request-billed GETs.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The bill breakdown and the flat-volume clue point cleanly at one place. Here's the reasoning:

**Where the money actually is, and what grew.** There are two heavy cost centers:
- **Ingest/dedup** — the majority of the *compute* bill (single-threaded hash-and-compare of every line against the recent-window index).
- **Archive reads** serving saved-searches — the single largest line item on the *whole* bill by object-storage GET request count.

The decisive filter is "cost up, ingested volume flat." Dedup work is a function of *incoming lines*. If ingested volume is flat, dedup's workload — and therefore its cost — is essentially flat too. It's big, but it's *steady*; it is not what climbed. So even though dedup dominates compute, it cannot be the thing driving a quarter-over-quarter *increase*, and optimizing it would be chasing a cost that didn't move.

The archive-read line is the one that both **is the largest** and can **grow independently of ingest volume**: it's billed per GET request, and request count is driven by query/read activity and by how many objects each read touches — neither of which is tied to how many logs you ingest. That's the cost/volume decoupling the team observed.

**The root cause of the read cost.** The infra note names it: archive **page granularity has never been tuned since launch — pages are small**, so a single saved-search read *fans out into many small GET requests*. Object storage charges per request, so small pages multiply the request count on every read. This is amplification baked into the storage layout, and it compounds as stored data and saved-search usage grow.

## The single highest-leverage fix

**Increase the archive page (chunk) size written to object storage, so each saved-search read resolves in far fewer GET requests.**

Why this beats the alternatives:

- **Magnitude** — it directly attacks the single largest line item on the bill (reads by request count). Coarser pages collapse the GET fan-out roughly in proportion to the size increase (e.g., 8× larger pages ≈ ~⅛ the request count for the same scan), which is a first-order cut, not a trim.
- **It matches the symptom** — read-request billing is exactly the cost that can rise while ingest volume stays flat. Fixing it addresses *what actually grew*.
- **Tractability** — it's a storage-layout/config change plus re-chunking going forward, not a rearchitecture. Low risk, no correctness change to the pipeline's semantics.
- **Durability** — it removes the amplification at the source, so every future read benefits and the line item stops scaling pathologically with usage.

Why the two untried compute ideas are weaker levers:
- **Parallelizing dedup across threads** reduces *latency*, not total core-hours — the same work spread over more threads generally costs the same or more. And it targets a cost that didn't rise (flat volume → flat dedup).
- **Batching dedup over a wider window** changes dedup effectiveness and adds recent-window index cost; again it targets steady compute, not the line item that climbed, and carries correctness/memory tradeoffs.

Both optimize the biggest *compute* slice, but that slice isn't the source of the increase and isn't the biggest *bill* line. The page-size fix is the one change that hits the largest, fastest-growing, volume-decoupled cost at its root.

(One caveat to tune, not a blocker: larger pages mean each GET pulls more bytes, so for highly selective saved-searches you trade request cost for a little more bytes-read. Since the dominant billed dimension here is *request count* and pages are currently undersized from launch, raise page size to right-size it — increase substantially, then verify against read-byte cost rather than maximizing blindly.)

Worth noting: my recall pass primed me toward compute-side suspects (retry reprocessing, per-stage metrics), but the team's data overrides that anchor — the evidence points squarely at read-path GET amplification, not compute.

RECOMMENDATION: Increase the archive object-storage page/chunk size so saved-search reads fan out into far fewer GET requests, cutting the largest (read-request) line on the bill.
recommendation: Increase the archive object-storage page/chunk size so saved-search reads fan out into far fewer GET requests, cutting the largest (read-request) line on the bill.
